feedback.text: As an avid supporter of the Stop Destroying Videogames campaign/consumer movement, I believe The Digital Fairness Act would be an even more effective set of measures to protect citizens against abuse in the digital and market spaces, and to ensure the conducting of ethical and resposible business by the corporate sector, if it also included the issue of videogames being made unplayable after they have been sold to customers. Companies like Sony, Microsoft, Nintendo, and others in and outside the videogame industry, shouldn't be allowed to undermine the concept of ownership and of selling a good in-exchange for money by selling videogames on their platforms as one-time purchase goods but then writing in their terms of service that they are only selling a "license" for customers to have access to their videogames, which more often then not, can be taken away for any or no reason, effectively stealing back what they have supposedly "sold" and not refunding or conpensating the customer. They should also not be able to sell a videogame that is dependent on their private servers to function (online-only videogames, videogames which only work properly when connected to online servers, etc.) without having an end-of-life plan that leaves the videogame in a reasonably playable state or another reasonable option for people who wish to preserve the game or would like to enjoy it in the future Basically, customers shouldn't have their goods effectively destroyed remotely by the owners of the IP, just like the owners of IKEA shouldn't have the right to destroy the furniture I have taken off their hands in exchanged for my money. If the power between sellers and buyers is unbalanced, it will just result in the deterioration of business and commerce overtime. And on a less immediate time-scale, I believe art has the right to be preserved by those who care about it and I believe it is parammount that we preserve our history for the future before it is lost.
